title: Weld Acceptance: Applicable Codes and Standards
narrationScript: Finding an indication is technique. Dispositioning it is law. The moment you characterize a reflector, the question becomes, accept or reject, and that answer never comes from your opinion. It comes from the governing code, the construction specification, and your written procedure, in that chain. A weld that is perfectly acceptable on a building frame might be rejectable in a reactor nozzle, not because the steel cares, but because the rulebooks differ. So a Level Two has to know whose rules apply and how each family of rules thinks. There are two broad philosophies. Workmanship criteria ask whether the welder controlled the process. They reject on echo amplitude compared to a reference, and on indication length, with limits that scale with wall thickness. They are deliberately conservative and simple to apply. Fitness for service criteria, the fracture mechanics approach, ask a different question. Will this flaw grow or fail in service. They care about flaw height through the wall, length, and character, and they demand accurate sizing, often with advanced techniques. Most production weld UT runs on workmanship rules, so master those first. Take the pressure equipment family. Codes such as the ASME boiler and pressure vessel code put the how in one book and the how good in another. The method article tells you to calibrate a distance amplitude correction curve from side drilled holes at one quarter, one half, and three quarters of the thickness in a basic calibration block. The curve becomes your primary reference level, one hundred percent DAC. You scan hot, typically several decibels above reference so nothing is missed, then drop back to reference to evaluate. The referencing construction code then sets the acceptance logic, and the pattern is consistent. Indications characterized as cracks, lack of fusion, or incomplete penetration are rejectable regardless of amplitude. Other indications that exceed the reference level are rejected when their length exceeds a limit tied to wall thickness, on the order of six millimeters for thin walls and growing with section size. There is usually a recording threshold too, commonly a percentage of DAC, below which nothing is documented. Now the structural family. Codes such as the structural welding code for steel buildings and bridges work in pure decibel arithmetic. You measure the indication level in decibels, subtract the reference level you established on the standard reflector, and subtract an attenuation factor that compensates for sound path, typically two decibels per inch of path beyond the first inch. The result is an indication rating, and the code tables convert that rating, together with weld thickness, probe angle, and whether the joint is statically or cyclically loaded, into accept, reject, or repair. Same physics, completely different bookkeeping, and the two systems do not interchange. International practice, in standards such as the ISO weld examination series, layers it differently again. One standard sets testing levels that scale rigor with the joint, and a companion sets acceptance levels keyed to reference curves from side drilled holes or notches. The lesson is the same everywhere. Identify the governing edition, read the acceptance clause, and apply only that clause. Whatever the family, your mechanics are identical. Maximize the indication, bring gain to the evaluation level, and compare amplitude to the reference. Size length with the six decibel drop. Walk the probe along the weld until the signal falls to half its peak height, mark, do the same on the other end, and measure between marks. Record everything the code asks. Indication number, datum location along and across the weld, sound path, leg, computed depth, peak amplitude versus reference, characterization, probe angle and frequency, calibration block, and your name and level. If it was rejected and repaired, the repaired weld is re-examined with the same procedure and the same criteria, and the report shows both examinations. Last, the ethics, because acceptance work is where they bite. Gain is calibrated. Nudging it down to slide an indication under the line is falsification, full stop. The procedure revision and code edition on the report must be the ones actually used. And the report itself is a legal record that outlives you, the welder, and probably the plant. Write it so a stranger can re-find every indication from your numbers alone. That is what evaluation means. Measure honestly, compare to the written rule, document completely, and let the code make the call.
imagePrompt: An inspector at a fabrication-shop workbench marks indication locations on a weld map clipboard beside a flaw detector displaying a DAC curve, with a calibration block, scale rule, and dog-eared code book on the bench.

sections.heading: Two Philosophies of Acceptance
sections.body:
Detection is technique; disposition is law. Accept/reject never comes from the technician's judgment in isolation — it comes from the **governing code or specification**, invoked by contract and implemented through your written procedure. The same indication can pass on one job and fail on another because the rulebooks differ, so the first question on any weld is: **whose rules apply, and which edition?**

Two philosophies dominate:

- **Workmanship criteria** ask, did the welder control the process? They reject on **echo amplitude versus a reference level** and on **indication length** (limits scaling with thickness), with planar characterizations often rejectable outright. Simple, conservative, and the basis of most production weld UT.
- **Fitness-for-service (fracture-mechanics) criteria** ask, will this flaw fail in service? They evaluate **flaw height (through-wall), length, character, and location** against engineering analysis — demanding accurate sizing, often with TOFD/PAUT, under documents such as the ASME flaw-evaluation alternatives or API fitness-for-service practice.

A Level II must master the workmanship systems first and know when a job has invoked the other kind.

sections.heading: Pressure-Equipment Practice: DAC-Based Evaluation
sections.body:
In the pressure-equipment family, the **method** and the **acceptance** live in different books: codes such as ASME Section V, Article 4 define how to examine, while the referencing construction code (Section VIII, Section I, B31 piping) defines how good is good enough.

The working tool is the **DAC curve**, built from **side-drilled holes at T/4, T/2, and 3T/4** in a basic calibration block of like material and thickness. The curve is the **primary reference level (100% DAC)**. You scan at increased gain — commonly reference plus several dB — so small signals are not missed, then **return to reference gain to evaluate**.

The acceptance pattern is consistent across the family:

- Indications **characterized as cracks, lack of fusion, or incomplete penetration are rejectable regardless of amplitude**.
- Other indications **exceeding the reference level** are rejected when their **length exceeds a thickness-dependent limit** — on the order of 6 mm (1/4 in.) for thin walls, growing stepwise with section size per the governing table.
- A **recording threshold** (commonly a stated percentage of DAC, e.g. 20–50% per the procedure) sets what must be documented and investigated.

Amplitude is always stated **relative to reference** — "50% DAC" or "−6 dB" — never as raw screen height.

sections.caption: A DAC curve from three side-drilled holes with reference, recording, and investigation levels, and two indications dispositioned against them.

sections.heading: Structural and International Practice
sections.body:
Structural codes such as **AWS D1.1** run the same physics through different bookkeeping: pure decibel arithmetic. The technician measures the **indication level** (dB), subtracts the **reference level** established on the standard reflector, and subtracts an **attenuation factor** that compensates for sound-path losses — conventionally **2 dB per inch (25 mm) of sound path beyond the first inch**. The result is an **indication rating**, and code tables convert that rating — together with weld thickness, probe angle, and whether the connection is **statically or cyclically loaded** — into accept, reject, or repair classes. Cyclic (fatigue) service gets the tighter tables.

International practice layers it differently again: standards such as **ISO 17640** define testing levels (A through D) that scale rigor with the joint, while companions such as **ISO 11666** set acceptance levels keyed to reference curves from side-drilled holes or notches at stated frequencies.

The systems share logic but **do not interchange**: an AWS indication rating cannot be compared to a percent-of-DAC number, and acceptance levels assume their own calibration reflectors. Identify the governing document and edition, read its acceptance clause, and apply only that clause — never criteria remembered from a previous job.

sections.caption: How the major code families frame weld UT acceptance (cited generically).
rows: Pressure equipment (ASME V Art. 4 + construction code)
DAC from SDHs at T/4, T/2, 3T/4
Cracks/LOF/IP reject outright; >100% DAC rejected over length limits tied to T
Indication rating = indication − reference − attenuation (≈2 dB/in. beyond 1 in.)
Rating vs tables by thickness, angle, static or cyclic loading
Testing level A–D + acceptance level vs reference
Amplitude and length limits per acceptance level
Compared against fracture-mechanics allowable flaw sizes

sections.heading: Recording, Reporting, and the Repair Loop
sections.body:
Whatever the family, the bench mechanics are identical. **Maximize** the indication, return gain to the **evaluation level**, and compare amplitude to reference. Size length with the **6 dB-drop** method: walk the probe along the weld until the signal falls to **half its peak screen height** (−6 dB), mark, repeat at the other end, and measure between marks — valid for reflectors longer than the beam width.

A recordable-indication entry that survives an audit includes:

- Indication number, weld ID, and **datum-referenced location** (distance along the weld and offset from centerline)
- **Sound path, leg, computed depth**, and surface distance
- **Peak amplitude versus reference** (% DAC or dB) and the gain used
- **Characterization** (planar/volumetric; suspected type) and measured **length**
- Probe angle, frequency, and serial; instrument; **calibration block**; couplant; procedure and revision
- Examiner's name, certification level, and signature with date

Rejected welds enter the **repair loop**: excavate, repair, then **re-examine the repair with the same procedure and criteria**, with both examinations on the record. Final reports also capture scan limitations and any areas of restricted coverage.

sections.caption: Disposition flow for a weld indication — from detection at scanning gain through geometry verification, recording, code evaluation, and the repair/re-examination loop.

sections.title: The procedure rules — and gain is sacred
sections.body: Apply the **written procedure and the code edition named in it** — never criteria remembered from another job, and never a blend of two systems. Calibrated gain is part of the legal record: nudging gain down to slide an indication under an acceptance line is **falsification**, and an evaluation done at the wrong sensitivity is void. If field conditions force a deviation — surface, access, temperature — stop and route it through the Level III, not around them.

figures.title: DAC reference, recording, and evaluation levels

interactive.title: Disposition the shift's indications
interactive.intro: You are evaluating a 30 mm single-V production butt weld to an amplitude-based (DAC) procedure: reference = 100% DAC from side-drilled holes, scanning gain = reference + 6 dB, recording level = 50% DAC. Work through the calls a Level II must make.

steps.prompt: First pass: a sharp signal peaks at 110% DAC, plots mid-wall on the left fusion face, holds steady amplitude over 12 mm of weld length, and collapses when you orbit. What do you do?
options: Reduce gain 6 dB so it reads below DAC and keep scanning
Record it, characterize it as suspected lack of fusion, and evaluate it as rejectable under the procedure's criteria
Accept it — 12 mm is less than half the wall thickness
steps.feedback: Sharp envelope, fusion-face position, steady length, orbit-sensitive: a planar LOF signature above reference. Under amplitude-based pressure-code criteria, characterized LOF is rejectable regardless of amplitude — and touching gain to hide it is falsification.

steps.prompt: Next, a twinkling cluster of small peaks reaches 15% DAC in the fill region. The procedure's recording level is 50% DAC. Your move?
options: Stop and write a full indication report to be safe
Raise the gain until the cluster crosses 50% and record it
No record required — it is below the recording level; keep scanning at the established scanning gain
steps.feedback: Recording thresholds exist to keep evaluation effort on signals that matter. A 15% DAC scattered cluster is below the documented threshold: no entry required. Raising gain to force a record corrupts the calibration exactly as much as lowering it to hide one.

steps.prompt: A strong root-area signal reaches 80% DAC. It plots just past the weld centerline, slightly beyond the far surface, and the mirror-image position on the opposite side shows the same signal at the same locus. The root is visually accessible. Best course?
options: Verify it as geometry — confirm plotting, compare both sides, and check the root profile — then note it as root bead, not a recordable defect
Reject the weld — anything near 100% DAC at the root is incomplete penetration
Ignore root signals entirely; the root always reflects
steps.feedback: Plotting beyond the far surface past centerline, symmetric from both sides, matching a visible bead: that is excess-penetration geometry. But it must be verified and noted, not assumed — a root crack plots at the centerline at/inside the surface, and 'the root always reflects' is how root cracks get shipped.

steps.prompt: A slag-like indication at mid-thickness evaluates at 130% DAC, so length now decides the call. How do you measure its length?
options: Measure between the points where the signal disappears completely into the noise
Walk the probe each way along the weld until the peak falls to half its maximum screen height (−6 dB), mark both ends, and measure between marks
Report the width of the probe footprint as the length
steps.feedback: The 6 dB-drop method defines the ends at half-amplitude — repeatable and what the code tables assume. Vanish-into-grass endpoints overstate length and depend on noise; the probe footprint has nothing to do with the reflector.

steps.prompt: The weld is repaired where you rejected the LOF. What closes the loop, and which report survives an audit?
options: A one-line note: 'repair re-checked, looks good'

Re-examine the repair with the same procedure and criteria; report both examinations with datum-referenced locations, amplitudes vs reference, sound paths and legs, probe and calibration details, and your name, level, and signature
Only the welder's visual sign-off is needed after repair
steps.feedback: Repairs are re-examined to the original procedure and acceptance criteria, and the record must let a stranger re-find every indication from the numbers alone. Anything less is not an NDT report.

checkQuestions.question: An indication peaks at 25% full screen height where the DAC curve sits at 50% FSH. Its amplitude relative to reference is about:
options: −3 dB (71% of DAC)
−6 dB (50% of DAC)
+6 dB (200% of DAC)
−12 dB (25% of DAC)
checkQuestions.explanation: ΔdB = 20·log₁₀(25/50) = 20·log₁₀(0.5) ≈ −6 dB; half the reference amplitude is 50% of DAC, 6 dB below reference.

checkQuestions.question: What fundamentally separates workmanship acceptance criteria from fitness-for-service (fracture-mechanics) criteria?
options: Workmanship criteria apply only to GTAW welds
Workmanship rejects on amplitude and length versus a reference; fitness-for-service evaluates measured flaw size (especially height) against engineering analysis of service failure
Fitness-for-service criteria are always less conservative paperwork-wise but require no sizing
Workmanship criteria ignore indication length entirely
checkQuestions.explanation: Workmanship rules judge process control via reference-amplitude and length limits; FFS rules ask whether the actual flaw, accurately sized through-wall, threatens the component in service.

checkQuestions.question: Under typical pressure-equipment (DAC-based) acceptance criteria, an indication characterized as a crack that peaks at only 40% DAC is:
options: Acceptable, because it is below the reference level
Acceptable if shorter than 6 mm
Rejectable — indications characterized as cracks, LOF, or IP are unacceptable regardless of amplitude
Recorded but never rejected
checkQuestions.explanation: Amplitude and length limits apply to other reflector types; planar characterizations (crack, LOF, IP) are rejectable outright because their orientation can hide severity from the amplitude.

checkQuestions.question: In AWS D1.1-style evaluation, the attenuation factor subtracted when computing the indication rating exists to:
options: Compensate for sound-path attenuation, conventionally about 2 dB per inch of path beyond the first inch
Correct for couplant temperature
Penalize welds in cyclic service
Convert percent DAC into decibels
checkQuestions.explanation: Longer sound paths lose more energy; the standardized attenuation factor levels the comparison so the rating reflects the reflector, not the distance to it.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of data makes an indication record auditable and repeatable?
options: Weld ID and a sketch of the screen
Peak screen height in millimeters and the welder's name
Probe brand, couplant color, and ambient temperature only
Datum-referenced location, amplitude versus reference, sound path/leg/depth, length, characterization, probe and calibration details, and the examiner's identity and level
checkQuestions.explanation: The test of a report is whether a stranger can relocate and re-evaluate every indication from the numbers alone — that requires location, amplitude relative to reference, geometry data, characterization, and traceable equipment/personnel details.

references: Method and calibration practice per codes such as ASME Section V, Article 4 (cited generically)
Acceptance criteria frameworks per construction codes such as ASME Section VIII / B31, AWS D1.1, and ISO 17640/11666 (cited generically)
Fitness-for-service flaw evaluation practice per documents such as API 579 (cited generically)
